topics.name: Introduction to function
required_content: A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. Defined once, called many times.
Advantages: code reusability, modularity, easier debugging, abstraction of logic
Without functions: same logic copy-pasted 10 places = 10 places to fix when a bug is found
common_mistakes: Thinking functions are optional — without them, code becomes unmaintainable beyond 50 lines
Defining the function but forgetting to call it — definition alone does nothing

topics.name: Function declaration and invoking
required_content: Parameter: variable in the function definition. Argument: actual value passed when calling. function add(a, b) — a,b are parameters. add(3, 5) — 3,5 are arguments.
return stops function execution and sends value back. Without return, function returns undefined.
Function declaration is hoisted — can call before it appears in code. Function expression is NOT hoisted.
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | Function Declaration | Function Expression || Syntax | function name() {} | const name = function() {} || Hoisting | Yes — call before define | No — must define first || Name | Always has name | Can be anonymous || Use case | General purpose functions | Callbacks, conditional definitions
common_mistakes: Confusing argument and parameter — parameter is the placeholder, argument is the real value
Forgetting return — the function does the work but the caller gets undefined

topics.name: Function with Parameter
required_content: Single parameter: function double(n) { return n * 2; }. Arrow shorthand: const double = n => n * 2.
Multiple parameters separated by comma. JS does not enforce argument count — missing args become undefined, extra args are ignored.
N number of parameters: use rest parameter ...args which collects all into an array. Or use the arguments object (old way, not available in arrow functions).
common_mistakes: Using arguments object inside arrow functions — it doesn't exist there, use ...rest instead
Passing too few arguments — missing params become undefined, causing NaN or errors silently

topics.name: Arrow function
required_content: Arrow functions: shorter syntax introduced in ES6. const fn = (params) => expression. If single expression, return is implicit.
Key difference: arrow functions do NOT have their own 'this'. They inherit 'this' from the surrounding lexical scope.
Arrow functions cannot be used as constructors (no new keyword), have no arguments object, and cannot be used as generator functions.
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | Traditional Function | Arrow Function || Syntax | function name() {} | const name = () => {} || this binding | Own this (dynamic) | Lexical this (from parent) || arguments object | Available | Not available — use ...rest || Use as constructor | Yes (new keyword) | No — throws TypeError || Hoisting | Yes (declaration) | No || Best for | Methods, constructors | Callbacks, array methods
common_mistakes: Using arrow function as object method — 'this' will refer to outer scope (window/undefined), not the object
Forgetting parentheses for zero-parameter arrow functions — () => is required, not just =>

topics.name: Recursive function
subtopics: Understanding the base case and recursive case
required_content: Recursion: a function that calls itself to solve smaller versions of the same problem.
Base case: the condition that stops recursion. Without it — infinite recursion → stack overflow.
Recursive case: the part that calls itself with a smaller/simpler input moving toward the base case.
common_mistakes: Missing base case — causes 'Maximum call stack size exceeded' (stack overflow)
Base case never reached — ensure recursive call moves toward base case (n-1, not n+1)

topics.name: Function Chaining
required_content: Function chaining: calling multiple methods on the same object in one line. Works when each method returns 'this' or the object itself.
Common in JS: 'hello world'.toUpperCase().split(' ').reverse().join('-') — each method returns a new string/array.
Limitation: if any method in the chain returns undefined or null, the next call throws TypeError. Hard to debug — error points to chain line not the failing method.
common_mistakes: Chaining on a method that returns undefined — breaks the chain with 'Cannot read property of undefined'
Long chains without intermediate variables — impossible to debug which step failed

topics.name: Important functions
required_content: First-class functions: in JS, functions are values — they can be stored in variables, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions.
Pure function: same input always gives same output, no side effects. Impure function: depends on or modifies external state (Math.random(), console.log are impure).
IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression): (function() { ... })() — runs immediately, creates private scope, avoids polluting global namespace.
Currying: transforming f(a,b,c) into f(a)(b)(c). Each call returns a new function waiting for the next argument.
Generator function: function* with yield. Returns an iterator. Pauses at each yield, resumes on .next() call.
HOF (Higher Order Function): takes a function as argument OR returns a function. map, filter, reduce are HOFs.
common_mistakes: Thinking callback = async — callbacks are just functions passed as arguments, they can be sync or async
Curried function vs partially applied function — currying always produces single-argument functions at each step

topics.name: Scope
subtopics: Types of scope: global & local(functional & block)
required_content: Scope determines where a variable is accessible. JS has 3 scope types: global, function, and block.
Global scope: declared outside any function/block — accessible everywhere. Risk: name collisions, accidental overwrites.
Function scope: var declared inside a function — only accessible within that function.
Block scope: let and const declared inside {} (if, for, while) — only accessible within that block.
common_mistakes: var ignores block scope — a var inside an if/for block leaks into the enclosing function
Thinking global variables are always safe — they persist forever and can be overwritten from anywhere

topics.name: Closure
subtopics: Intro to closure with example & a real life use case
required_content: Closure: an inner function retains access to its outer function's variables even after the outer function has returned. The inner function 'closes over' the outer scope.
Lexical scope: scope is determined by where the function is WRITTEN in the code, not where it is called.
Scope chaining: when JS looks up a variable, it searches current scope → outer scope → outer-outer scope → global. Stops at first match.
Practical use: data privacy (counter, bank balance) — outer variable is inaccessible from outside but inner function can use it.
common_mistakes: Classic closure-in-loop bug with var: for(var i=0; i<3; i++) { setTimeout(() => console.log(i)) } — prints 3,3,3. Fix: use let
Thinking closure copies the variable — it captures a REFERENCE, so the value seen is the current value at call time

topics.name: let and const
subtopics: Difference between let and const vs var
required_content: var was the only option before ES6 — function-scoped, hoisted, re-declarable. This caused many bugs.
let: block-scoped, not re-declarable in same scope, in Temporal Dead Zone before declaration.
const: same as let but must be initialized at declaration and cannot be reassigned. Object/array contents CAN still be mutated.
Rule: use const by default. Use let when you need to reassign. Never use var in modern JS.
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | var | let | const || Scope | Function | Block | Block || Hoisting | Yes (undefined) | Yes (TDZ — error if accessed) | Yes (TDZ — error if accessed) || Redeclaration | Allowed | Error | Error || Reassignment | Allowed | Allowed | Error || Must initialize | No | No | Yes || Use today | Avoid | For mutable values | Default choice
common_mistakes: const with objects — thinking const makes object immutable. It only prevents reassignment of the variable
Accessing let/const before declaration — Temporal Dead Zone throws ReferenceError unlike var which gives undefined

topics.name: Number
required_content: toFixed(n): rounds to n decimal places, returns string. Used for displaying prices: (9.999).toFixed(2) → '10.00'
toPrecision(n): formats to n significant digits total. (123.456).toPrecision(4) → '123.5'
Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER = 2^53 - 1 = 9007199254740991. Beyond this, integers lose precision in JS.
Number.isNaN() vs global isNaN(): Number.isNaN(undefined) → false. isNaN(undefined) → true. Always use Number.isNaN().
topics.required_code:
// Number methods
const price = 9.9999;
console.log(price.toFixed(2));        // '10.00' (string)
console.log(price.toPrecision(3));    // '10.0'
console.log((1234).toExponential(2)); // '1.23e+3'
console.log((255).toString(16));      // 'ff' (hex)
console.log((7).toString(2));         // '111' (binary)

// Number properties
console.log(Number.MAX_VALUE);           // 1.7976931348623157e+308
console.log(Number.MIN_VALUE);           // 5e-324 (smallest positive)
console.log(Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER);    // 9007199254740991
console.log(Number.MIN_SAFE_INTEGER);    // -9007199254740991
console.log(Number.POSITIVE_INFINITY);   // Infinity
console.log(Number.isInteger(4.0));      // true
console.log(Number.isFinite(Infinity));  // false
common_mistakes: toFixed() returns a string, not a number — parseFloat(price.toFixed(2)) if you need a number for math
Floating point precision: 0.1 + 0.2 !== 0.3 in JS. Use toFixed or multiply to integers before math

topics.name: Math
required_content: Math is a built-in object (not a class — cannot use new Math()). All methods are static.
Math.random() returns float between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). For random integer 1-10: Math.floor(Math.random() * 10) + 1
Math.ceil() rounds UP always. Math.floor() rounds DOWN always. Math.round() rounds to nearest.
topics.required_code:
console.log(Math.abs(-7));        // 7
console.log(Math.ceil(4.1));      // 5
console.log(Math.floor(4.9));     // 4
console.log(Math.round(4.5));     // 5
console.log(Math.round(4.4));     // 4
console.log(Math.max(3, 7, 1));   // 7
console.log(Math.min(3, 7, 1));   // 1
console.log(Math.pow(2, 8));      // 256
console.log(Math.sqrt(144));      // 12
console.log(Math.PI);             // 3.141592653589793

// Random integer between min and max (inclusive)
function randomInt(min, max) {
    return Math.floor(Math.random() * (max - min + 1)) + min;
}
console.log(randomInt(1, 6));  // dice roll simulation
common_mistakes: Math.round(4.5) = 5 but Math.round(-4.5) = -4 (rounds toward +infinity) — not symmetric
Using Math.random() for security — it is NOT cryptographically secure. Use crypto.getRandomValues() instead

topics.name: Date
required_content: new Date() creates current date/time. Date stores milliseconds since Jan 1, 1970 UTC (Unix epoch).
GOTCHA: getMonth() returns 0-11, not 1-12. January = 0, December = 11. Always add 1 when displaying.
Date.now() returns current timestamp in ms — faster than new Date().getTime() for performance measurement.
common_mistakes: getMonth() returning 0 for January — always add 1 before displaying month to users
Parsing date strings with new Date('DD-MM-YYYY') — format inconsistent across browsers. Use YYYY-MM-DD

topics.name: Regex in Javascript
required_content: Regex (Regular Expression): pattern for matching text. Two ways to create: /pattern/flags or new RegExp('pattern', 'flags').
Key characters: . (any char), * (0 or more), + (1 or more), ? (0 or 1), ^ (start), $ (end), [] (character class), \d (digit), \w (word char), \s (whitespace).
Flags: g (global — find all), i (case-insensitive), m (multiline).
Methods: regex.test(str) → boolean. str.match(regex) → array. str.replace(regex, newStr) → new string.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the g flag — without it, match() returns only the first result
Using new RegExp() with backslashes — need double backslash: new RegExp('\\d+') for \d+

topics.name: String
subtopics: length, slice, substring, substr, replace, replaceAll, toUpperCase, toLowerCase, concat, split, indexOf, lastIndexOf, startsWith, endsWith, search, trim, charAt, at, charCodeAt
required_content: Strings are immutable — methods return new strings, never modify original. str[0] = 'X' silently fails.
slice(start, end): negative index counts from end. slice(-3) = last 3 chars. substring doesn't support negative.
Template literals (backticks): support multiline strings and ${expression} interpolation — preferred over concatenation.
at(-1) is the modern way to get last character. str[str.length-1] is the old way.
common_mistakes: Trying to mutate string with index: str[0] = 'j' — silently does nothing in non-strict mode
substr() vs substring() vs slice() — substr is deprecated. Use slice (supports negative) or substring

topics.name: Introduction to Array
required_content: Array: ordered collection of values. Can hold mixed types. Zero-indexed — first element is index 0.
Arrays are objects in JS — typeof [] === 'object'. Use Array.isArray() to check.
for...of iterates VALUES directly — cleaner than traditional for loop when index not needed.
common_mistakes: Accessing out-of-bounds index returns undefined (not an error) — can cause subtle bugs downstream
Using for...in on arrays — iterates string indices AND prototype properties, use for...of instead

topics.name: Array Methods
required_content: Mutating methods (change original): push, pop, shift, unshift, splice, reverse, sort. Non-mutating (return new): slice, map, filter, reduce, concat.
map vs forEach: map returns a new array. forEach returns undefined — use it for side effects only.
filter returns all matches as array. find returns first matching element (or undefined). some returns boolean (any match). every returns boolean (all match).
sort() default sorts as strings — [10, 2, 1].sort() = [1, 10, 2]. Always pass comparator for numbers: arr.sort((a,b) => a-b)
required_tables: Comparison: Method | Mutates? | Returns | Use for || push/pop | Yes | element/length | Add/remove from end || shift/unshift | Yes | element/length | Add/remove from start || splice | Yes | removed items | Insert/delete at position || slice | No | new array | Copy a portion || map | No | new array | Transform each element || filter | No | new array | Keep matching elements || reduce | No | single value | Accumulate to one value || find | No | element/undefined | First matching element || forEach | No | undefined | Side effects only
common_mistakes: sort() without comparator on numbers — sorts lexicographically: [10,2,1] becomes [1,10,2]
Using map when forEach is sufficient (or vice versa) — map for transformation, forEach for side effects

topics.name: Introduction to Object
required_content: Object: collection of key-value pairs. Keys are strings (or Symbols). Values can be any type including functions (then called methods).
Dot notation: obj.name (when key is known, valid identifier). Bracket notation: obj['name'] or obj[variable] (when key is dynamic or has spaces).
delete obj.property removes a property. Adding: just assign obj.newProp = value.
Optional chaining (?.) — safely access nested properties. user?.address?.city returns undefined instead of throwing if address is null/undefined.
common_mistakes: Using dot notation with dynamic keys — obj.variable gives you property named 'variable', not the value of variable
for...in iterates inherited prototype properties too — use hasOwnProperty() or Object.keys() to get own props only

topics.name: Object metadata
required_content: Every object property has 3 hidden attributes (descriptors): writable, enumerable, configurable — all true by default when you assign normally.
writable: false — property cannot be changed (silently fails in non-strict, throws in strict mode).
enumerable: false — property won't appear in for...in or Object.keys().
configurable: false — property descriptor cannot be changed, property cannot be deleted.
common_mistakes: Thinking writable:false makes nested objects immutable — it only prevents reassignment of that property reference
Confusing non-enumerable with non-existent — property still accessible directly, just hidden from iteration

topics.name: Object methods and enumerating properties
required_content: Object.keys(obj): array of own enumerable property names. Object.values(obj): array of values. Object.entries(obj): array of [key, value] pairs.
Object.assign(target, source): shallow copy/merge. Object.freeze(obj): deep immutability prevention (cannot add/change/delete).
Object.seal(obj): cannot add or delete properties, but CAN change existing values. Object.freeze is stricter — no changes at all.
Object.fromEntries(entries): inverse of Object.entries. Converts array of [key,val] pairs back to object.
required_tables: Comparison: Method | Can Add | Can Change | Can Delete || Normal object | Yes | Yes | Yes || preventExtensions | No | Yes | Yes || seal | No | Yes | No || freeze | No | No | No
common_mistakes: Object.assign does SHALLOW copy — nested objects are still referenced, not cloned. Use structuredClone() for deep copy
Object.freeze is also shallow — nested objects inside a frozen object are still mutable

topics.name: map and set
subtopics: includes creation from & to object & arrays respectively & vice versa as well
required_content: Map: key-value collection where keys can be ANY type (objects, functions, primitives). Maintains insertion order. Unlike plain objects, no prototype pollution.
Set: collection of UNIQUE values. Automatically removes duplicates. Maintains insertion order.
Map → Object: Object.fromEntries(map). Object → Map: new Map(Object.entries(obj)). Set → Array: [...set] or Array.from(set). Array → Set: new Set(array).
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | Object | Map || Key types | String/Symbol only | Any type || Key order | Not guaranteed (mostly) | Insertion order guaranteed || Size | Manual (Object.keys.length) | .size property || Iteration | for...in (includes prototype) | .forEach, for...of || Performance (frequent add/delete) | Slower | Faster
common_mistakes: Using map.get(key) with an object key — must use the SAME object reference, not an equal-looking object
Checking Set membership with indexOf — use set.has(), not array methods

topics.name: Spread and Rest
required_content: Spread (...): EXPANDS an iterable into individual elements. Used in function calls, array literals, object literals.
Rest (...): COLLECTS remaining elements into an array. Used in function parameters and destructuring.
Same syntax (...) but opposite purpose — spread expands, rest collects. Context determines which it is.
common_mistakes: Spread creates SHALLOW copy — nested objects still reference original. Use structuredClone() for deep copy
Rest parameter must be last — function(a, ...rest, b) is a SyntaxError

topics.name: Destructuring in JS
required_content: Destructuring: unpack values from arrays or properties from objects into variables in one clean line.
Array destructuring: position-based. Object destructuring: name-based (order doesn't matter).
Default values in destructuring: const { name = 'Guest' } = user — uses 'Guest' if name is undefined.
Rename while destructuring: const { name: userName } = user — creates variable userName with value of user.name.
common_mistakes: Nested destructuring when property is null/undefined — throws TypeError. Use optional chaining or default: { address: { city } = {} }
Object destructuring is name-based not position-based — const { b, a } = { a: 1, b: 2 } gives b=2, a=1 (correct)

topics.name: this keyword
required_content: 'this' refers to the object that is executing the current function. Its value depends on HOW the function is called, not where it's defined.
Global context: this = window (browser) or global (Node). Object method: this = the object. Arrow function: this = inherited from outer scope.
Event handler: this = the DOM element that fired the event (unless arrow function used).
common_mistakes: Using arrow functions as object methods — arrow functions don't have own 'this', they inherit from outer scope
Detaching a method from its object — const fn = obj.method; fn() loses 'this'. Use .bind() to preserve it

topics.name: call, apply, and bind method
required_content: All three explicitly set 'this' for a function. call() and apply() invoke immediately. bind() returns a new function.
call(thisArg, arg1, arg2): arguments passed individually. apply(thisArg, [arg1, arg2]): arguments as array.
bind(thisArg): returns a new permanently-bound function. Useful for event handlers and callbacks.
required_tables: Comparison: Method | Invokes immediately | Args format | Returns || call | Yes | Individual: fn.call(this, a, b) | Return value of fn || apply | Yes | Array: fn.apply(this, [a, b]) | Return value of fn || bind | No | Individual: fn.bind(this, a, b) | New bound function
common_mistakes: Using apply when you want call — apply takes array, call takes individual args. Easy to mix up
bind returns a function — forgetting to call it: const fn = greet.bind(obj); fn() not just greet.bind(obj)

topics.name: Hoisting
subtopics: Hoisting with let, var and const
required_content: Hoisting: JS engine moves declarations to the top of their scope during compilation phase — before any code runs.
var hoisting: declaration hoisted, initialized to undefined. Accessing before assignment gives undefined (no error).
let/const hoisting: declaration hoisted but NOT initialized. Accessing before declaration throws ReferenceError — this gap is the Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ).
Function declarations are fully hoisted (both declaration and body). Function expressions and arrow functions follow variable hoisting rules.
common_mistakes: Thinking let/const are not hoisted — they ARE hoisted but in TDZ. The difference is initialization
Relying on var hoisting intentionally — always declare variables before use for readable code

topics.name: Object constructor
subtopics: Understanding this keyword in object constructor
required_content: Object constructor: a function used as a template to create multiple similar objects using the new keyword.
Convention: constructor function names start with Capital Letter (Person, Car, BankAccount).
When called with new: 1) new empty object created, 2) 'this' set to that object, 3) function runs (sets properties), 4) object returned automatically.
common_mistakes: Calling constructor without new — 'this' refers to global object, properties set on window (bug in strict mode: TypeError)
Adding methods inside constructor — each instance gets its own copy of the method. Put methods on prototype instead

topics.name: Prototypes in JavaScript
subtopics: Adding properties and methods to the prototype
required_content: Every JS object has a hidden [[Prototype]] link to another object. When a property isn't found on the object, JS looks up this chain.
Constructor.prototype: the object that will become [[Prototype]] of instances. instance.__proto__: the actual prototype of that instance. They point to the same object.
Add methods to prototype (not inside constructor) so ALL instances share ONE copy — memory efficient.
Prototype chain lookup: instance → Constructor.prototype → Object.prototype → null
required_diagrams: Prototype chain diagram: show 3 boxes in a vertical chain connected by [[Prototype]] arrows. Top box: rex instance (name, breed properties). Middle box: Dog.prototype (bark method, constructor). Bottom box: Animal.prototype (speak method). Arrow from bottom to Object.prototype, then to null.
common_mistakes: Adding methods inside constructor instead of prototype — 1000 instances = 1000 copies of same function in memory
Overwriting entire prototype: Dog.prototype = Animal.prototype (wrong) vs Dog.prototype = Object.create(Animal.prototype) (right)

topics.name: Polyfills in JavaScript
required_content: Polyfill: code that implements a modern feature for browsers that don't natively support it. Fills the gap — like spackle filling holes.
Pattern: check if feature exists → if not, implement it manually on the prototype.
Common polyfills: Array.prototype.flat, Array.prototype.includes, Object.assign, Promise.
common_mistakes: Not checking if feature exists before polyfilling — may override the native (better) implementation
Polyfilling on Object.prototype (not Array.prototype) causes issues — all objects would inherit the method

topics.name: Introduction to OOJS
required_content: OOP in JS is prototype-based, not class-based like Java/C++. ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over prototype system.
4 pillars of OOP in JS: Encapsulation (closures, classes), Inheritance (prototypes, extends), Polymorphism (method overriding), Abstraction (hiding implementation).
JS classes are just constructor functions under the hood — class syntax makes them look like traditional OOP languages.
common_mistakes: Thinking JS classes work like Java classes — JS classes are prototype-based under the hood, not classical inheritance
Forgetting that class is not hoisted like function declarations — cannot use class before it's defined

topics.name: Constructor function
required_content: Constructor function: uses new keyword, 'this' for properties, PascalCase name convention.
Factory function: regular function that creates and returns objects. No new keyword, no this confusion.
new keyword: creates new object, sets its prototype to Constructor.prototype, binds 'this', returns object automatically.
Factory functions are safer (no new keyword confusion) but don't set up prototype chain automatically.
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | Constructor Function | Factory Function || Invocation | new keyword required | Called like regular function || this usage | Uses this | No this needed || instanceof | Works correctly | Always false || Prototype chain | Set automatically | Not set || Risk | Forgetting new is bad | No such risk || Memory | Methods on prototype | Methods recreated each time
common_mistakes: Calling constructor without new in non-strict mode — 'this' is window, properties pollute global scope silently
Factory functions and instanceof — car instanceof createCar is always false since factory returns plain object

topics.name: ES6 classes
required_content: class keyword, constructor() method, methods defined directly in class body (auto-added to prototype).
extends keyword for inheritance. super() must be called first in child constructor before accessing 'this'.
static methods/properties: belong to the class itself, not instances. Call as ClassName.method(), not instance.method().
Getters (get) and setters (set): look like properties but execute functions. Useful for validation and computed properties.
Classes are NOT hoisted unlike function declarations — ReferenceError if used before declaration.
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | Classical Inheritance (Java/C++) | Prototypical Inheritance (JS) || Mechanism | Copy-based | Reference/delegation-based || Classes | Blueprint — instances copy class | Syntactic sugar over prototypes || Object creation | new creates new copy | new creates object with prototype link || Method lookup | In the class definition | Up the prototype chain || Flexibility | Rigid type hierarchy | Any object can inherit from any object
common_mistakes: Forgetting super() in child constructor — 'Must call super constructor in derived class before accessing this'
Calling static method on instance — rex.kingdom() fails. Must be Animal.kingdom() or Dog.kingdom()

topics.name: Introduction to Error Handling
subtopics:
Types of error handling
Syntax error
Runtime error
Logical error
required_content: 3 types: Syntax error (caught at parse time — typos, missing brackets). Runtime error (during execution — null.property, undefined is not a function). Logical error (runs without error but wrong output — hardest to find).
Error object properties: name (type of error), message (what went wrong), stack (where it happened — line numbers).
Custom errors: extend the Error class to create domain-specific errors with meaningful names.
common_mistakes: Catching errors silently (empty catch block) — swallows errors making debugging impossible
Using try-catch for logical errors — they won't throw, they just produce wrong output. Use console.log and debugger

topics.name: Re-throwing and Extending Errors
required_content: Re-throwing: catch only the errors you can handle — rethrow the rest so they propagate up to a handler that knows what to do.
Pattern: catch(err) { if (err instanceof SpecificError) { handle } else { throw err; } }
Built-in error types: Error (base), TypeError, RangeError, ReferenceError, SyntaxError, URIError, EvalError.
common_mistakes: Catching Error (base class) when you only want NetworkError — instanceof Error is true for ALL error types
Re-throwing without throw keyword — just writing err in catch block does nothing, must use throw err

topics.name: AJAX
required_content: JS is single-threaded but async operations (network, timers, I/O) are handled by browser Web APIs — they run in background, results come back via callback queue.
AJAX (Asynchronous JavaScript and XML): technique to fetch data from server without reloading the page. Modern apps use JSON not XML.
XMLHttpRequest (XHR): old API for making HTTP requests. Still used but replaced by fetch() in modern code.
common_mistakes: Accessing xhr.responseText before readyState === 4 — data is not complete yet
Not handling xhr.onerror — network failures would be swallowed silently

topics.name: Promise
required_content: Promise: object representing eventual completion or failure of async operation. States: pending → fulfilled (resolve) or rejected (reject).
async/await: syntactic sugar over Promises. async function always returns a Promise. await pauses until Promise resolves.
JSON.stringify(obj): JS object → JSON string (for sending). JSON.parse(str): JSON string → JS object (for receiving).
common_mistakes: Using await outside async function — SyntaxError. Every function using await must be marked async
Not catching rejected promises — unhandled rejections crash Node apps and show warnings in browser

topics.name: Browser API
required_content: localStorage: persists data even after browser close (until cleared). sessionStorage: cleared when tab closes. Both store string key-value pairs — use JSON.stringify/parse for objects.
fetch(): modern Promise-based HTTP API. Returns Response object — must call .json() or .text() to get body.
Geolocation API: navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition() — user must grant permission. Async operation.
History API: history.pushState(), history.back(), history.forward() — manipulate browser history without page reload (used in SPAs).
common_mistakes: Storing objects directly in localStorage — localStorage.setItem('obj', {}) stores '[object Object]'. Always JSON.stringify
Not checking response.ok after fetch — fetch only rejects on network error, not on 404/500 HTTP errors

topics.name: Window Object
required_content: window is the global object in browsers — all global variables and functions are properties of window. console.log === window.console.log.
setTimeout(fn, ms): runs fn once after ms milliseconds. Returns ID. clearTimeout(id) cancels it.
setInterval(fn, ms): runs fn repeatedly every ms milliseconds. Returns ID. clearInterval(id) stops it.
window.location: current URL info. location.href to navigate. location.reload() to refresh. window.screen: screen dimensions.
common_mistakes: setInterval without clearInterval — creates memory leak, interval runs forever even if component/page destroyed
Using alert/prompt/confirm in production — they block JS execution completely and look unprofessional

topics.name: Visualisation of DOM and It's Working
subtopics: Visualisation of DOM and It's Working
required_content: DOM (Document Object Model): browser's in-memory representation of HTML as a tree of objects. JS can read and modify this tree.
Every HTML element becomes a Node. Types: Element nodes (tags), Text nodes (text content), Attribute nodes.
Changes to DOM immediately update the rendered page. This is how React, Vue, Angular work — they manipulate the DOM.
required_diagrams: DOM tree diagram: show hierarchical tree. Root: 'document' node. Child: 'html' element. Two children of html: 'head' and 'body'. Head has 'title' child with text node 'My Page'. Body has 'h1' (text: 'Hello'), 'p' (text: 'World'), 'ul' with two 'li' children. Each node shown as labeled box, connected by lines. Clean white background.
common_mistakes: Confusing DOM with HTML file — DOM is browser's live in-memory object. HTML is just source text. They can diverge (JS changes DOM without changing HTML file)
Running DOM code before DOM loads — put script at bottom of body or use DOMContentLoaded event

topics.name: Methods of DOM - Part 1
required_content: getElementById: fastest, returns single element or null. getElementsByClassName/TagName: returns live HTMLCollection (updates automatically).
querySelector: returns FIRST match using CSS selectors — most flexible. querySelectorAll: returns static NodeList of ALL matches.
HTMLCollection is live (updates when DOM changes). NodeList from querySelectorAll is static (snapshot at query time).
required_tables: Comparison: Method | Returns | CSS Selectors | Live? | Multiple || getElementById | Single element/null | No | N/A | No || getElementsByClassName | HTMLCollection | No | Yes | Yes || getElementsByTagName | HTMLCollection | No | Yes | Yes || querySelector | Single element/null | Yes | No | No || querySelectorAll | Static NodeList | Yes | No | Yes
common_mistakes: Calling forEach on HTMLCollection directly — it's not an array. Use Array.from() or [...collection] first
querySelector('#id') slower than getElementById('id') — use getElementById for ID lookups

topics.name: Methods of DOM - Part 2
required_content: innerHTML: gets/sets HTML content (parses HTML tags). innerText: gets/sets visible text (respects CSS display:none). textContent: gets/sets all text (ignores CSS, faster).
innerHTML with user input is XSS vulnerability — never do element.innerHTML = userInput. Use textContent instead.
setAttribute(name, value): sets any attribute. getAttribute(name): gets attribute value as string.
common_mistakes: Using innerHTML for text-only content — use textContent which is faster and safe from XSS
Mixing .src property (returns full URL) with getAttribute('src') (returns original attribute value)

topics.name: Methods of DOM - Part 3
required_content: Prefer classList over style for toggling visual states — keeps style in CSS, logic in JS.
createElement + appendChild: create elements programmatically. More efficient than innerHTML for dynamic content.
DOM navigation: parentNode, childNodes, firstChild, lastChild, nextSibling, previousSibling, children (elements only).
common_mistakes: Using style.background-color (hyphenated) — JS needs camelCase: style.backgroundColor
appendChild vs append: appendChild takes single Node. append() can take multiple nodes AND strings

topics.name: Event in JS
subtopics: Javascript event listeners & ways to adding it
required_content: Events: actions that happen in browser — user click, keypress, form submit, page load, scroll, resize.
addEventListener(type, handler, useCapture): preferred way. Can add multiple handlers. Can remove. useCapture=true for capturing phase.
Event propagation: Capturing (top-down window→element) → Target → Bubbling (bottom-up element→window). By default listeners fire in bubbling phase.
Event delegation: attach one listener on parent, handle events from all children. Efficient for dynamic lists.
preventDefault(): stops default browser action (form submit, link navigate). stopPropagation(): stops event from bubbling up.
required_diagrams: Event propagation diagram: show nested boxes — window (outermost), document, body, div, button (innermost). Show two phases with arrows: Capturing phase arrows pointing DOWN from window to button (left side). Bubbling phase arrows pointing UP from button to window (right side). Label 'useCapture: true' for capturing, 'default (bubbling)' for the upward phase.
common_mistakes: removeEventListener with anonymous function — can't remove it. Store handler in variable and pass same reference
Calling event.stopPropagation() thinking it stops other handlers on SAME element — use stopImmediatePropagation() for that

topics.name: NodeList vs. HTML Collection
required_content: HTMLCollection: returned by getElementsByClassName/TagName. Live — automatically reflects DOM changes. Only contains Element nodes.
NodeList: returned by querySelectorAll (static) or childNodes (live). Can contain Element AND Text nodes.
Convert to array for full array methods: Array.from(collection) or spread [...collection].
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | HTMLCollection | NodeList (querySelectorAll) | NodeList (childNodes) || Source method | getElements* | querySelectorAll | .childNodes || Live/Static | Live | Static | Live || Node types | Elements only | Elements only | All nodes (text, elements) || forEach support | No (convert first) | Yes | Yes || filter/map/reduce | No (convert first) | No (convert first) | No (convert first)
common_mistakes: Assuming querySelectorAll NodeList is live — it's a static snapshot. Add new elements and length won't change
Calling .filter() directly on NodeList — must convert to array first: [...nodeList].filter(...)

topics.name: Import/Export
subtopics: Importing and exporting a function and variable
required_content: Module: a separate file with its own scope. Variables/functions are private by default — must explicitly export to share.
CommonJS (Node.js): module.exports = ... and require(). Synchronous loading. Used in Node.
ESM (ES Modules): export and import keywords. Async loading. Used in browsers and modern Node. Use type='module' in script tag.
Default export: one per file, import with any name. Named export: multiple per file, import with exact name in {}.
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | CommonJS | ES Modules || Syntax (export) | module.exports = | export / export default || Syntax (import) | require() | import ... from ... || Loading | Synchronous | Asynchronous || Environment | Node.js (primarily) | Browsers + modern Node || Dynamic import | require() anywhere | import() function (returns Promise) || File extension | .js | .js or .mjs
common_mistakes: Forgetting type='module' on script tag — ES module import/export won't work in browser without it
Exporting default and trying to import with {} — default imports don't use curly braces

topics.name: console
required_content: console.table(data): displays arrays/objects as a formatted table in DevTools — far more readable than console.log for arrays of objects.
console.error() prints in red with stack trace. console.warn() prints in yellow. Both have dedicated filters in DevTools.
console.assert(condition, message): prints message only if condition is FALSE. Great for quick sanity checks during development.
common_mistakes: Leaving console.log in production — use a logging library that can be disabled in prod, or remove them before deploy
Using console.log for error states — use console.error so it appears in error filters and includes stack trace

topics.name: Execution Context
required_content: JS engine has two parts: Memory Heap (stores objects/variables) and Call Stack (tracks function execution — LIFO).
Browser provides Web APIs (setTimeout, fetch, DOM events) that run outside JS engine. When done, callback goes to queue.
Event Loop: checks if Call Stack is empty → takes from Microtask Queue (Promises) first → then Callback Queue (setTimeout, DOM events).
Microtask queue has HIGHER priority than Callback/Macrotask queue — Promise .then() runs before setTimeout callback.
required_diagrams: Event Loop diagram: show 4 boxes. Left: 'Call Stack' (vertical stack with function frames). Middle-top: 'Web APIs' box (setTimeout, fetch, DOM). Middle-bottom: two queues side by side — 'Microtask Queue (Promises)' and 'Callback Queue (setTimeout, events)'. Right: 'Memory Heap'. Show circular arrow from queues back to Call Stack labeled 'Event Loop checks: stack empty? → microtask first → then callback'. Clean white background, color-coded boxes.
common_mistakes: Thinking setTimeout(fn, 0) runs immediately — it goes to callback queue, runs AFTER all synchronous code AND microtasks
Not knowing Promises have higher priority than setTimeout — this explains many async ordering bugs

topics.name: Strict mode
required_content: Strict mode: opt-in mode that catches common JS mistakes and prevents unsafe features. Enabled with 'use strict' at top of file or function.
What strict mode prevents: undeclared variables, duplicate parameters, deleting non-configurable properties, this in global context is undefined (not window).
ES6 modules and classes are in strict mode automatically — no need to add 'use strict'.
common_mistakes: Placing 'use strict' in the middle of a file — it only works when it's the FIRST statement
Thinking strict mode slows down code — modern JS engines optimize strict mode code BETTER than sloppy mode

topics.name: Debugging
required_content: debugger statement: pauses code execution at that line when DevTools is open — like a breakpoint in code.
Chrome DevTools Sources tab: set breakpoints by clicking line numbers. Step through code with F10 (step over), F11 (step into), Shift+F11 (step out).
VS Code debugger: launch.json config, breakpoints in editor, watch variables, call stack view.
common_mistakes: Leaving debugger statement in production — code pauses for all users who open DevTools
Commenting out code to debug instead of using breakpoints — lose context of what the code does

topics.name: Performance tips
subtopics: Understanding the code organization and best pratices
Using const for declaring array and object
required_content: Garbage collection: JS automatically frees memory when objects are no longer reachable. V8 uses Mark-and-Sweep algorithm.
Memory leaks: objects that are still referenced but no longer needed. Common causes: forgotten setInterval, accidental global variables, detached DOM nodes, event listeners not removed.
Style guide essentials: camelCase for variables/functions, PascalCase for classes, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants, 2-space indentation, semicolons.
Automatic type coercion traps: 1 + '1' = '11' (string concat). 1 - '1' = 0 (numeric). Always use explicit conversion.
common_mistakes: Relying on JS type coercion — 0 == '' is true, 0 == false is true. Always use === and explicit conversions
Not monitoring memory in Chrome DevTools Memory tab — memory leaks are invisible without profiling
